mainmatter.p: Let { \mathopen {} \left ( A, \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}} and { \mathopen {} \left ( B, \beta \right ) \mathclose {}} be two \mathbb {L}-algebras; then a morphism of dcpos { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is a homomorphism of algebras if and only if it preserves the bottom element.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: It is clear that a homomorphism of algebras must preserve the bottom element. On the other hand, suppose that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } preserves the bottom element to check that the following diagram commutes:
Because both composites preserve the bottom element, it is enough to observe that they both take u \in TA to the supremum of the following semidirected subset:

mainmatter.p: A dcpo is called pointed when it has a bottom element \bot under the information order.

frontmatter.title: Suprema of semidirected subsets in pointed dcpos

mainmatter.p: A dcpo A is pointed if and only if it is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Suppose that A is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets. In particular, we may consider the supremum \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} of the empty set. This is evidently the bottom element, using the universal property of the supremum.

Conversely, suppose that A is pointed and let I \subseteq A be semidirected. We may replace I \subseteq A by the directed subset I' = I \cup { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bot \right \} \mathclose {}}; the inclusion I \subseteq I' is clearly cofinal, so the supremum of I' is also the supremum of I.

mainmatter.p: A pointed dcpo carries a (unique) \mathbb {L}-algebra structure.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The algebra structure is unique if it exists because \mathbb {L} is a Kock–Zöberlein monad, i.e. a monad for which the structure maps of algebras are left adjoint to the unit in the order-enrichment. Therefore, it suffices to show that there merely exists an algebra structure { \mathsf { L } {A} } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ A } for any pointed dcpo A.

We define the structure map \alpha to take u \in \mathsf { L } {A} to the supremum of the semidirected subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ x \in A \mid u = \eta _Ax \right \} \mathclose {}}, so we may write \alpha u = \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p]. It is not difficult to see that this assignment is continuous.
The unit law for the algebra is trivial. The multiplication law is verified as follows:

mainmatter.p: Let { \mathopen {} \left ( A, \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}} be a \mathbb {L}-algebra; then the underlying dcpo A is pointed.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: To define the bottom element of A, we choose \alpha { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}}. Fixing n \in A, we must check that \alpha { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}} \sqsubseteq n. Because \mathsf { L } is a Kock–Zöberlein monad and thus we have \alpha \dashv \eta _{A}, it is equivalent to observe that { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}} \sqsubseteq \eta _{A}n.

mainmatter.p: The category of \mathbb {L}-algebras and homomorphisms is (canonically) equivalent to the category of pointed dcpos and bottom-preserving morphisms of dcpos. It follows that pointed dcpos are monadic over dcpos.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Having a bottom element and being an algebra are both properties of dcpos, because these structures are uniquely determined. Therefore, we will argue that pointed dcpos with bottom-preserving morphisms form the same subcategory of \mathsf { dcpo } as the category of algebras with algebra-preserving maps. We have already seen that pointed structure and algebra structure are interchangeable:
To finish, we recall that a morphism of dcpos preserves algebra structure if and only if it preserves the bottom element.

mainmatter.p: By virtue of the monadicity result, we will refer to pointed dcpos and \mathbb {L}-algebras interchangeably.

We will deduce the closure of \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } under connected colimits using general results about monads. Then we use the results of Linton to deduce the cocompleteness of \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} }. Note that the proof establishing that the lift monad preserves connected colimits has an error in it. I have a fix for it, suggested by Ohad Kammar, but I haven't written it down yet here.
For any dcpo A, the following is a co-comma square in the posetal 2-category of dcpos, where \mathsf { L } takes a dcpo to its lift or Scott-open partial map classifier.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We fix another lax square of the following form:

We must show that there exists a unique morphism \mathsf { L } {A} \xrightarrow {d}D factoring the square above through { \mathopen {} \left \{ d_ \bot \sqsubseteq d_A \right \} \mathclose {}} as follows:

We will define \mathsf { L } {A} \xrightarrow {d}D to take a ^{ ? } : \mathsf { L } {A} to the least upper bound of the directed subset U = { \mathopen {} \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} \mathclose {}} \cup { \mathopen {} \left \{ d_A a \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} \mathclose {}} \subseteq D. As this assignment evidently has the correct factorization property, it remains just to check that it is continuous. Letting V be a directed subset of \mathsf { L } {A}, we compute:
For uniqueness, suppose that we have two morphisms \mathsf { L } {A} \xrightarrow {d,d'}D factoring our square in the sense described above and fix a ^{ ? } : \mathsf { L } {A}:

mainmatter.p: Let A_ \bullet be a diagram of dcpos indexed in a connected category \mathcal {I}. Then the lifting endofunctor \mathsf { L } on dcpos preserves the colimit of A_ \bullet.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let A_ \bullet \xrightarrow { \iota _ \bullet } { \mathopen {} \left \{ A_ \infty \right \} \mathclose {}} be a universal cocone; we must show that \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow { \mathsf { L } { \iota _ \bullet }} { \mathopen {} \left \{ \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } \right \} \mathclose {}} is universal as well, i.e. show any cocone \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {d_ \bullet } { \mathopen {} \left \{ D \right \} \mathclose {}} factors uniquely through \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow { \mathsf { L } { \iota _ \bullet }} { \mathopen {} \left \{ \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } \right \} \mathclose {}} in the following sense:

Using the universal property of \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } as a co-comma dcpo, we may define \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } \xrightarrow {d_ \infty }D to be the universal map determined by a certain lax square in the following configuration:

First we define { \mathbf {1} _{ } } \xrightarrow {{ h_ \bot }}{ D } to be the element d_i \bot determined by an arbitrary object i \in \mathcal {I}. To see that this is well-defined, fix i,j \in \mathcal {I} to check that d_i \bot =d_j \bot. As \mathcal {I} is connected, we may proceed by induction on a zigzag i \leadsto j \in \mathcal {I}; ultimately, this amounts to checking that for any span i \leftarrow k \rightarrow j in \mathcal {I}, we have d_i \bot =d_k \bot =d_j \bot. This follows from the strictness of lifted morphisms as well as the naturality of the cocone d_ \bullet.
Next we define { A_ \infty } \xrightarrow {{ h _{ A_ \infty } }}{ D } using the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ \iota _ \bullet }}{ A_ \infty }:

To arrange h _{ A_ \infty } and h_ \bot into the desired lax square, we must check that for all a_ \infty :A_ \infty we have h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } a_ \infty. Fixing arbitrary i \in \mathcal {I} and a \in A_i, it suffices to check that h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _{ i } a. As h_ \bot =d_i \bot and h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _ia = d_i \eta _{ A_i } a, our goal follows directly from the monotonicity of { \mathsf { L } {A_i} } \xrightarrow {{ d_i }}{ D }.

p.mark: The reasoning above is not correct, we do not know that we can restrict attention to a \in A_i. This is indeed the case, but need to make it explicit.

block.p: Thus we have the desired lax square and a unique morphism { \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ D } factoring it through the co-comma square that defines \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty }:

Our goal is to show that we may choose the universal map { \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow {{ d_ \infty }}{ D } corresponding to the cocone { \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow {{ d_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ D \right \} \mathclose {}} } to be the morphism h defined above. First we must prove that the following triangle commutes:
Using the universal property of the co-comma squares for each \mathsf { L } {A_i}, it suffices to check that two diagrams commute:

li.p: We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting using the strictness of lifted morphisms, this amounts to the established fact that h \bot = h_ \bot.
We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting with established equations, it suffices to observe that the outer diagram below commutes:

ol.p: Finally, we must check that any two morphisms { \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow {{ k,k' }}{ D } factoring { \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow {{ d_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ D \right \} \mathclose {}} } through { \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { L } { \iota _ \bullet } }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } \right \} \mathclose {}} } are equal. To that end, we will use the universal property of the co-comma square defining \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } once more to reduce this to checking that k,k' agree on \bot and on \eta _{ A_ \bullet }. The former follows directly from the strictness of \mathsf { L } { \iota _ \bullet }, as we have k \bot = k { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } \iota _ \bullet \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} =d_ \bullet \bot. For the latter, we must check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } =k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty }; by the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ \iota _ \bullet }}{ A_ \infty }, it suffices to check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet = k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet. By naturality of the unit, these are both equal to d_ \bullet \circ \eta _{ A_ \bullet }.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathbb {L} = { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } , \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} be the lifting monad on dcpos; the Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } is closed under connected colimits, and these colimits are created by the forgetful functor { \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { U } ^{ \mathbb {L} } }}{ \mathsf { dcpo } }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows from the following facts:

ol.li: The category of dcpos is (even constructively) known to be cocomplete.
The forgetful functor { \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { U } ^{ \mathbb {L} } }}{ \mathsf { dcpo } } creates whatever colimits \mathsf { L } preserves.

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb {L} = { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } , \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} on dcpos has all small coproducts.
This is the first part of Corollary 2 of Linton's Coequalizers in categories of algebras.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The coproduct of lift-algebras is computed as a reflexive coequalizer, whose existence we have already established. Given an index set I and a diagram { I } \xrightarrow {{ X _{ \bullet } }}{ \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } }, the colimit of the following reflexive pair is the coproduct of { I } \xrightarrow {{ X _{ \bullet } }}{ \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } } as shown by Linton:

frontmatter.title: The category of lift-algebras is cocomplete

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb {L} = { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } , \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} on dcpos is cocomplete.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This follows because \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } under connected colimits and coproducts

mainmatter.p: Let { \mathopen {} \left ( A, \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}} be a \mathbb {L}-algebra; then the underlying dcpo A is pointed.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: To define the bottom element of A, we choose \alpha { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}}. Fixing n \in A, we must check that \alpha { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}} \sqsubseteq n. Because \mathsf { L } is a Kock–Zöberlein monad and thus we have \alpha \dashv \eta _{A}, it is equivalent to observe that { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}} \sqsubseteq \eta _{A}n.

mainmatter.p: The category of \mathbb {L}-algebras and homomorphisms is (canonically) equivalent to the category of pointed dcpos and bottom-preserving morphisms of dcpos. It follows that pointed dcpos are monadic over dcpos.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Having a bottom element and being an algebra are both properties of dcpos, because these structures are uniquely determined. Therefore, we will argue that pointed dcpos with bottom-preserving morphisms form the same subcategory of \mathsf { dcpo } as the category of algebras with algebra-preserving maps. We have already seen that pointed structure and algebra structure are interchangeable:
To finish, we recall that a morphism of dcpos preserves algebra structure if and only if it preserves the bottom element.
